post.title: On The Meaning Of Life
post.body:
I'm here to talk about the meaning of life.

I briefly mentioned it in my happiness video, but I wasn't very specific.

Another thing to know about me is that I highly value definitions. Even though I don't always explain things very precisely in conversation, I care a lot about having clear definitions and being specific. That probably comes from taking classes in deductive and inductive logic. I really enjoy that kind of thinking, so I naturally value precise definitions.

Because of that, I wanted to make a separate video about the meaning of life. I touched on it before, but I don't think I explained it well enough. I was also really tired while recording those videos, so I kept using the word "happiness" to substitute for a lot of different ideas.

So here's my actual answer.

My meaning of life is simply to enjoy life.

That ties directly into my belief in living in the moment.

This idea mostly developed through taking philosophy classes. The topic of the meaning of life came up a lot, especially in my Philosophy of Happiness course.

For the longest time, I considered myself an optimistic nihilist.

That idea originally came from a Kurzgesagt video that YouTube randomly recommended to me.

Not just a nihilist, but an optimistic nihilist.

The idea was that life doesn't necessarily have some predetermined cosmic meaning, and that's okay. I didn't think I had a specific purpose. I just wanted to enjoy life.

One day, I was talking with someone I consider a friend of virtue—a very close friend whom I highly respect. We don't always agree on everything, but I respect him a lot.

He believes that everyone has a meaning in life.

He believes everyone is searching for meaning.

I disagreed.

I said, "I don't think I have one."

That fit with the way I understood nihilism.

Later, I had a completely separate conversation with another close friend after a rave.

The topic somehow came up again.

I mentioned what my other friend had said—that everyone has a meaning in life.

Again, I said I didn't think I had one.

I explained that I honestly didn't care about having some grand purpose.

I just wanted to enjoy life.

A lot of the things I do are simply because I think they're fun.

I'll admit that way of thinking was also influenced by another friend I really respect.

I genuinely do a lot of things simply because I enjoy doing them.

If I don't enjoy the journey, I probably wouldn't do it.

For example, I genuinely enjoy school.

I enjoy learning computer science just as much as I enjoy learning philosophy.

That's one of the reasons I'm pursuing a Philosophy major.

It's simply fun.

So my mindset was always, "I don't really have a purpose. I just want to enjoy life."

Then that friend asked me one question that completely changed how I thought about it.

He said,

"Isn't your purpose just enjoying life?"

That completely shifted my perspective.

I realized that maybe I do have a meaning in life.

It's simply to enjoy life.

To live in the moment.

To be grateful.

And to make a positive impact on other people.

Gratitude is still a huge part of that.

Without gratitude, I don't think I'd actually enjoy anything.

If I wasn't grateful for what I already have, I'd constantly be focused on what I don't have instead.

For me, gratitude is what allows me to truly appreciate life.

When I think about it, almost everything I do is because I enjoy doing it.

Sometimes people ask, "If your purpose is just to enjoy life and live in the moment, then why do you still pursue goals? Doesn't that contradict what you're saying?"

Maybe it does.

That's something I'm still open to refining, and I'd love to have more discussions about it.

But personally, I think life would be pretty boring without goals.

Goals make life fun.

I enjoy school, but it would be kind of boring to just take random classes forever.

I want to work toward earning a degree.

Obviously, I also can't completely ignore money.

Money is important.

Part of the reason I chose Computer Science is because it provides financial stability.

But something like Philosophy?

That's honestly just for fun.

I'm also incredibly fortunate that my education is essentially free.

If it weren't for that, my decisions might look very different.

I'm very grateful to be in the position I'm in.

This ended up being an even shorter video than the others.

I just wanted to make sure I talked about the meaning of life somewhere because it's something that's important to me.

Again, if you disagree with anything I've said—or if you just want to discuss it more—please leave a comment or talk to me in real life.

I'd genuinely love to hear your thoughts.

Anyway, that's about it.
